title: Diameter Subscriber Data Accounting
spl:
sourcetype="stream:diameter" command_code=271
| eval total_bytes=acct_input_octets+acct_output_octets
| eval total_MB=round(total_bytes/1048576, 2)
| stats sum(total_MB) as total_data_MB, count as session_count by calling_station_id, origin_host
| sort -total_data_MB
| head 100
description: Aggregates Diameter accounting records to track data usage per subscriber and session, enabling detection of high-usage anomalies, billing reconciliation, and capacity planning.
value: Revenue assurance teams reconcile Diameter accounting data with billing CDRs, catch anomalous high-usage subscribers before overage complaints, and provide capacity planning with per-PGW throughput visibility.
implementation: Configure Splunk App for Stream to capture Diameter Accounting-Request (ACR, command_code 271) and Accounting-Answer (ACA, command_code 271) messages. The fields `acct_input_octets` and `acct_output_octets` provide byte counts per session. Correlate with `calling_station_id` (subscriber MSISDN/IMSI) to build per-subscriber usage profiles. Set alerts for subscribers exceeding data thresholds.
visualization: Bar chart (top 20 subscribers by data usage in MB), Table (calling_station_id, origin_host, total_data_MB, session_count — sortable), Line chart (aggregate data volume trend over 7 days), Single value (total Diameter accounting sessions).
knownFalsePositives: Planned Diameter work, HSS profile pushes, and roaming test campaigns can depress success rates in narrow windows. Compare to the PCRF and STP maintenance calendar.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Splunk App for Stream (Splunkbase 1809) v8.0+ with the Stream Forwarder deployed on a mirror/tap that sees Diameter Rf/Gy accounting traffic between the PGW/GGSN and the OCS/OFCS (Online/Offline Charging System).
• The Diameter accounting interface uses command_code 271 (ACR/ACA — Accounting-Request/Answer). The AVPs (Attribute-Value Pairs) that carry byte counters are `Accounting-Input-Octets` (AVP code 363) and `Accounting-Output-Octets` (AVP code 364), mapped to Stream fields `acct_input_octets` (download from subscriber perspective) and `acct_output_octets` (upload). The `calling_station_id` AVP (code 31) carries the subscriber's MSISDN.
• Know your accounting record types: ACR has a sub-type (`Accounting-Record-Type` AVP): 1=EVENT_RECORD, 2=START_RECORD, 3=INTERIM_RECORD, 4=STOP_RECORD. For usage aggregation you need INTERIM and STOP records (types 3 and 4) — START records contain zero byte counters. If the Stream parser does not extract the record type, you can filter by `acct_input_octets > 0` to exclude START records.
• Billing system correlation: understand whether your mediation platform uses the same Diameter accounting records you are capturing. If so, your Splunk aggregation should reconcile with the billing CDR totals (within the tolerance of capture completeness).
• Index and retention: accounting records for billing reconciliation may need longer retention (90–180 days). Create `index=telecom_accounting` with appropriate retention policies. Volume: approximately 0.5–2 KB per ACR × number of active sessions × interim update frequency (typically every 15–60 minutes per session). A network with 500K concurrent sessions and 30-min interim updates ≈ 24M records/day ≈ 12–48 GB/day.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
In the Splunk Stream app, create or reuse a Diameter stream that includes ACR messages. If you have a general Diameter stream from UC-5.10.1, you can reuse it. If creating a dedicated stream for accounting:

| Setting | Value |
|---------|-------|
| Protocol | Diameter |
| Name | `diameter_accounting` |
| Filter | `command_code == 271` |
| Fields | `command_code`, `result_code`, `origin_host`, `calling_station_id`, `acct_input_octets`, `acct_output_octets`, `session_id`, `application_id` |
| Sourcetype | `stream:diameter` |
| Index | `telecom_accounting` |

Verify accounting data is arriving:
```spl
index=telecom_accounting sourcetype="stream:diameter" command_code=271 earliest=-15m
| stats count by origin_host
| where count > 0
```
Each `origin_host` should correspond to a PGW or GGSN in your mobile core. If you see zero events, verify the mirror captures the Rf/Gy interface traffic.

Verify byte counter fields are populated:
```spl
index=telecom_accounting sourcetype="stream:diameter" command_code=271 earliest=-15m
| where isnotnull(acct_input_octets) AND acct_input_octets > 0
| stats count, avg(acct_input_octets) as avg_dl_bytes, avg(acct_output_octets) as avg_ul_bytes
```
If `acct_input_octets` is always null, the Stream parser may not extract these AVPs. Check the Stream protocol configuration and ensure Diameter AVP extraction depth is sufficient.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

**Primary search — Top subscribers by data usage (daily report):**
```spl
index=telecom_accounting sourcetype="stream:diameter" command_code=271 earliest=-24h
| where acct_input_octets > 0 OR acct_output_octets > 0
| eval total_bytes=acct_input_octets + acct_output_octets
| eval total_MB=round(total_bytes/1048576, 2)
| eval download_MB=round(acct_input_octets/1048576, 2)
| eval upload_MB=round(acct_output_octets/1048576, 2)
| stats sum(total_MB) as total_data_MB sum(download_MB) as dl_MB sum(upload_MB) as ul_MB count as acr_count dc(session_id) as sessions by calling_station_id, origin_host
| eval dl_ul_ratio=round(dl_MB/if(ul_MB>0, ul_MB, 0.01), 1)
| sort -total_data_MB
| head 100
```

Understanding this SPL: We filter for ACR records with non-zero byte counters (excluding START records). The `total_bytes` eval sums download and upload octets per record, then converts to megabytes. The `stats` command aggregates across all accounting records for each subscriber (`calling_station_id` = MSISDN). The `dl_ul_ratio` identifies asymmetric usage — a ratio >10 suggests heavy streaming/download, while a ratio <1 suggests unusual upload patterns (possible data exfiltration or tethering). The `dc(session_id)` counts distinct PDP/PDN sessions per subscriber — multiple sessions could indicate tethering or multi-APN usage.

**Anomaly detection — subscribers exceeding plan thresholds:**
```spl
index=telecom_accounting sourcetype="stream:diameter" command_code=271 earliest=-24h
| where acct_input_octets > 0
| eval total_MB=round((acct_input_octets + acct_output_octets)/1048576, 2)
| stats sum(total_MB) as daily_MB by calling_station_id
| lookup subscriber_plans.csv calling_station_id OUTPUT plan_limit_MB plan_name
| eval usage_pct=if(isnotnull(plan_limit_MB) AND plan_limit_MB>0, round(daily_MB*100/plan_limit_MB, 1), null())
| where daily_MB > 5000 OR usage_pct > 80
| sort -daily_MB
```

Understanding this SPL: Compares daily usage per subscriber against their plan limit from a lookup. Subscribers using >80% of their daily allocation (extrapolated from monthly limit / 30) or exceeding an absolute 5 GB/day threshold are flagged. This catches heavy users before they trigger rate-limiting or overage charges.

**Billing reconciliation — aggregate volume by PGW (weekly):**
```spl
index=telecom_accounting sourcetype="stream:diameter" command_code=271 earliest=-7d
| where acct_input_octets > 0
| eval total_GB=round((acct_input_octets + acct_output_octets)/1073741824, 3)
| stats sum(total_GB) as total_GB count as acr_records dc(calling_station_id) as unique_subscribers by origin_host
| eval avg_gb_per_sub=round(total_GB/unique_subscribers, 3)
```

Understanding this SPL: Weekly aggregate per PGW/GGSN that can be compared against the billing mediation system's CDR totals. If the Splunk total and the billing system diverge by >5%, there may be missing accounting records (dropped SCTP packets, Stream forwarder overload, or PGW misconfiguration).

Schedule as Alert: the anomaly detection search runs every 4 hours with a 24h lookback. Trigger when daily_MB > 10000 (potential abuse). The billing reconciliation runs weekly for finance review.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) Pick 3 known subscribers (MSISDNs): one low-usage, one medium, one heavy. Compare their Splunk-computed `total_data_MB` for a specific day against the CDR records from your mediation/billing platform. Values should align within 10% — differences arise from timing (ACR interim updates vs. CDR close-of-session) and capture completeness.

(b) Verify the `calling_station_id` format: it should be the subscriber's MSISDN in E.164 format (e.g. `+4712345678`) or IMSI. If the field shows a different format or is null, the Diameter AVP extraction may need adjustment.

(c) Check for double-counting: if the Stream Forwarder captures both the ACR request and ACA response, byte counters may appear in both. Verify that `command_code=271` alone correctly targets only ACR messages, or add a direction filter if available.

(d) Validate the `subscriber_plans.csv` lookup if used: ensure the `calling_station_id` key format matches what appears in the Diameter data.

(e) Cross-check aggregate volume: compare `| stats sum(total_GB) | eval total_TB=round(total_GB/1024, 2)` for a week against your network's reported data throughput from the PGW performance counters or the RAN backhaul meters.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard (recommended layout, named "Mobile Core — Subscriber Data Accounting"):
• Row 1 — Single-value tiles: "Total data volume (24h) in TB", "Active subscribers (24h)", "Accounting records processed (24h)", "Top subscriber usage (24h) in GB".
• Row 2 — Bar chart: top 20 subscribers by daily data usage (MB), with color-coded bars for DL vs UL.
• Row 3 — Table: calling_station_id, total_data_MB, dl_MB, ul_MB, dl_ul_ratio, sessions, acr_count. Drilldown on MSISDN to show hourly usage breakdown.
• Row 4 — Timechart: aggregate data volume per PGW over 7 days with daily granularity, for capacity trending.

Alerting:
• High usage (daily_MB > 10 GB): alert fraud/abuse team — potential SIM farm, tethering abuse, or compromised account.
• Billing reconciliation drift (Splunk total vs. billing > 5%): alert revenue assurance team.
• Missing accounting records (expected PGW shows zero ACRs for >1 hour): alert core network team — PGW accounting interface may be down.

Runbook (owner: Revenue Assurance / Core Network):
1. **Heavy user flagged**: Pull the subscriber's session history (`dc(session_id)`). Multiple concurrent sessions suggest tethering or SIM cloning. Check the subscriber's plan type and contact history. If legitimate, no action; if suspicious, escalate to fraud team.
2. **Billing reconciliation gap**: Compare Splunk ACR count with CDR count from mediation. If Splunk shows fewer, check for Stream Forwarder packet drops (`index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd "stream" "dropped"`) or mirror saturation. If Splunk shows more, check for duplicate captures.
3. **PGW stops sending accounting**: Check PGW process status and Diameter peer connectivity. If the Rf/Gy interface is down, subscribers on that PGW are using data without accounting — revenue leakage.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **`acct_input_octets` and `acct_output_octets` are always null** — The Stream parser may not be extracting these Diameter AVPs. Verify that the Stream Forwarder version supports full Diameter AVP extraction (requires Stream 8.0+). As a workaround, check if the raw event (`_raw`) contains the byte counter values and use `rex` to extract them.

• **`calling_station_id` is null** — This AVP may not be present in all ACR messages depending on your core vendor's implementation. Check the raw events. Some vendors use `Subscription-Id` (AVP 443) with sub-AVPs instead. You may need a custom field extraction.

• **Data volumes seem impossibly high for some subscribers** — Check for duplicate ACR records (same `session_id` and timestamp). Also verify you are not summing both ACR requests and ACA responses (which echo the same byte counters). Filter to only request messages if needed.

• **Zero events from a specific PGW** — The PGW may not have Diameter accounting enabled, or may be using RADIUS accounting instead (common in older 3G cores). Verify the PGW's accounting interface configuration.

• **Interim records overcount usage** — Diameter ACR interim updates report cumulative byte counters for the session, not incremental. If you sum multiple interim records for the same session, you will overcount. Use `| dedup session_id sortby -_time` to keep only the latest (highest) counter per session, or use STOP records only.
grandmaExplanation: We add up data volume from the mobile core’s per-subscriber accounting feed so unusual usage or missing billing shows up before finance or customers are surprised.
